---
title: "Dual Boot Ubuntu and Windows 7 after removing Windows 10 and Ubuntu-14.04.3"
date: 2016-04-24
forum: New to Ubuntu
---

### Post by BhuvneshKapoor on 2016-04-24
Hi,

**I would first bring to your attention the problem I am facing:**
 
It is so that few months back I created a separate partition of say 10GB on my system and installed Ubuntu - 14.04.3 (64bit) on that and at that time I had Windows 7 (64bit) installed on my system. But I think I didn't made it 'dual boot' because when I turned on my system Windows was getting loaded by default. 
Then few weeks later I got upgrade message from Windows to upgrade to Windows 10. I did chose to upgrade and currently I have Windows 10 installed on my system and in a separate partition there is Ubuntu - 14.04.3 installed as well, but I don't have idea how to access it !

[B]My present requirement:

[/B]Now I want to have a dual boot system in which:
1) I want to install Windows 7 in place of Windows 10
2) Ubuntu as a second OS. Since I am a beginner in Ubuntu you can suggest the version or distro which I can start with. 

So please guide me how can I make fresh installation of above OS(s) to my system with Dual Boot feature ? 

Thanking You,
Bhuvnesh

P.S: If some information give by me is not clear please ask for clarification.

---

### Post by oldfred on 2016-04-24
What hardware?
Or is it UEFI or BIOS.
How you boot installer for both Windows & Ubuntu UEFI or BIOS is then how it installs and you must be consistent, or both UEFI or both BIOS.
Windows 7 defaults to BIOS, you have to copy to flash drive and edit it slightly to make it UEFI.
Ubuntu installer is both, and if system is UEFI, the boot of the USB drive will have two options.

If older hardware, 14.04 should be fine, but very new hardware needs latest software most of which is now in 16.04. I have had no issues with 16.04, but that can depend on exactly what system you have. Video & Internet are often the issues where default drivers in distribution may or may not work. Most installs work much better with wired Ethernet, even if you normally use wireless.

---

### Post by Rocky_Bennett on 2016-04-24
oldfred, that is not absolutely true. I am running an Asus Z-97 motherboard that is hybrid, a lot of motherboards are hybrid. I have my Windows 10 installed in UEFI mode and my Ubuntu installed in BIOS classic. So your advice is not 100% correct. I hang around a few Linux sites where a lot of users installed Linux distros in the same manner as me on a lot of different brands of hardware. Hybrid is pretty common.

---

FXaL/z9X/8A38T/AOIortKKP7Rxf/Pxh9Qw38iP/9k=[/IMG]

It seems its a BIOS hardware.
Rocky_Bennett how to check if its a hybrid ?

---

### Post by oldfred on 2016-04-24
You can use mixed/hybrid boot, but then can only boot from UEFI boot menu or one time boot key often f10 or f12, but my Asus is f8.

Hybrid is only with UEFI as UEFI has CSM, otherwise is it BIOS only. 
CSM - UEFI Compatibility Support Module (CSM), which emulates a BIOS mode
All new systems that came with Windows 8 or later pre-installed are UEFI. A few older ones may be. Almost all UEFI are dual boot or what Rocky is calling as hybrid. A few are UEFI class 3 and do not offer BIOS boot, but those were usually very limited UEFI/systems (low cost) and intended as Windows only and 32 bit UEFI boot.

 UEFI Advantages
[http://askubuntu.com/questions/647303/uefi-or-legacy-which-is-advised-and-why/647604#647604](http://askubuntu.com/questions/647303/uefi-or-legacy-which-is-advised-and-why/647604#647604)
[http://askubuntu.com/questions/446968/legacy-vs-uefi-help](http://askubuntu.com/questions/446968/legacy-vs-uefi-help)
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unified_Extensible_Firmware_Interface](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unified_Extensible_Firmware_Interface)
GPT or MBR
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1625285](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1625285) 

Windows only boots with UEFI from a gpt partitioned drive. So if older system, you have to keep MBR for Windows. I used MBR for XP but had Ubuntu on a separate gpt drive for several years until I turned off XP.
      
 GPT Advantages (older but still valid)  see post#2 by srs5694:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1457901](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1457901)
[https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/GUID_Partition_Table#Advantages_of_GPT](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/GUID_Partition_Table#Advantages_of_GPT)
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unified_Extensible_Firmware_Interface](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unified_Extensible_Firmware_Interface)

---

### Post by UbuntuAddict99 on 2016-04-26
[COLOR=#000000]
Hello BhuvenshKapoor,

First off welcome to Ubuntu and the free  realm of Linux, now the issue you have is quite easy to solve and do not  worry i had the same issue about six months ago. Now all you have to  do is re-install Windows 7 over Windows 10. Then after that you just  need to run Ubuntu of off a live CD/DVD and install a program called  Boot Repair. And repair GRUB. Or you can try and fix GRUB through  Windows via this link URL:[/COLOR][COLOR=#000000][http://itsfoss.com/no-grub-windows-linux/](http://itsfoss.com/no-grub-windows-linux/) (The link is for Windows 10 but it never hurts to attempt it on Windows 7)

Now this should work with minimal issues. But Windows 7 supports dual booting with GRUB in my experience Windows 8 and 8.1 up and 10 all have issues due
to "Secure Boot" Microsoft’s silent attempt at stopping Linux. 

So a rundown.

1) Re-install Windows 7 over Windows 10 because that is what you want.
2) Either re-install Linux Ubuntu, or attempt to fix GRUB with a Live CD/DVD with Boot Repair.
3) Attempt to fix GRUB through Windows with the provided URL as written above.

Or the easy option.

4) [/COLOR][COLOR=#ff0000]_**OPTIONAL!**_[/COLOR][COLOR=#000000] Run Linux Ubuntu as a Virtual Machine if your PC supports VT-X. and also do not forget to check your BIOS/UEFI for Secure Boot if it is there just disable it.

I hope this has helped/answered your question. I have been where you were before and no need to listen and read endless BIOS and UEFI articles. the issue is simple once you know the solution. Happy Linuxing.

P.S. Feel free to ask more questions or even hey, answer some! 8-)

"Linux Ubuntu the final frontier!"
                                          -UbuntuAddict99
[/COLOR][URL="http://itsfoss.com/no-grub-windows-linux/"]
[/URL]

---

### Post by BhuvneshKapoor on 2016-05-29
Hi [COLOR=#000000]UbuntuAddict99,


Much Thanks for your guidance.
Following  the procedure given by you I have installed Windows 7 after that I  tried to repair GRUB by that didn't work out for some reasons then I  went for re-installation of the Ubuntu 14.04.3, however I faced error  while installation. The snapshot of error is at link:  [https://www.dropbox.com/sh/7q4n6g4763x2rkp/AAB1Cl-QwVaJtAl3tZdtBVVda?dl=0](https://www.dropbox.com/sh/7q4n6g4763x2rkp/AAB1Cl-QwVaJtAl3tZdtBVVda?dl=0)

Please suggest how to re-install the Ubuntu 14.04.3 ?

Best Regards,
Bhuvnesh Kapoor
[/COLOR]

---

### Post by oldfred on 2016-05-29
You can only have 4 primary partitions with BIOS/MBR.
Windows 7 default install is BIOS/MBR, but it can be modified on flash drive to boot installer in UEFI mode.
Do you already have an extended partition, which counts as one of the 4 primary partitions. And you can only have one extended partition per drive.


Post this:
sudo parted -l

       My laptop already has 4 primary partitions: how can I install Ubuntu?
[http://askubuntu.com/questions/149821/my-laptop-already-has-4-primary-partitions-how-can-i-install-ubuntu](http://askubuntu.com/questions/149821/my-laptop-already-has-4-primary-partitions-how-can-i-install-ubuntu)
Good advice on how to handle all four primary partitions used. - srs5694
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1686440](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1686440)
Be sure to create recovery DVD(s) first. And a Windows repair CD.

---

### Post by BhuvneshKapoor on 2016-06-16
Hi oldfred,

I have 3 primary partitions for windows and fourth one (extended if its so called) for Ubuntu. 
But as you can see from the snapshot in my previous post, when i try to reinstall the Ubuntu it doesn't show the partition table so i am not sure where it is going to install the OS!

-Bhuvnesh
